name: Filipino
register: Reverent contemporary Filipino, matching the Magandang Balita Biblia (Good News in Filipino) tradition — the most-loved and widely-read Filipino Bible, warm and accessible — anchored in the deeply beloved liturgical 'Ama namin' of the Lord's Prayer (Ang Panginoong Pananalangin) that every Filipino Christian knows by heart. Elevated but never archaic; the intimacy of a child speaking to a cherished father.
quote_convention: Straight double quotation marks “ ” for speech, single ‘ ’ for quotes nested within quotes (MBB usage).
poetry_convention: Poetic books (Psalms, Proverbs, prophetic oracles) set as indented verse lines, one poetic colon per line, with continuation/run-over lines further indented; Hebrew parallelism preserved line-for-line.

glossary.gloss: our Father who is in Heaven
glossary.note: Anchored to Ang Biblia / MBB (Mt 6:9) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "aming Amang nasa Langit". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)

Anchored to Ang Biblia / MBB (Mt 6:9 / Rom 1:7) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "Ama namin". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)
Definite filial name; 'Ama' with the article 'ang.' Same warm root as 'Ama namin,' not the generic deity word.

glossary.gloss: Heavenly Father (Father who is in Heaven)
glossary.note: Anchored to Ang Biblia / MBB (Mt 6:14) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "Amang nasa Langit". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)
Aramaic 'Abba' retained then glossed with the filial 'Ama' — exactly the MBB rendering of Rom 8:15 / Gal 4:6 ('Abba, Ama!'), so it is already familiar and reverent.
NON-FILIAL contrast term. Generic deity word; use only where English keeps 'God' as God-as-God. Must never stand in for 'Ama namin.'

NON-FILIAL contrast term for the covenant name; traditionally set in small caps (PANGINOON) as in Ang Biblia. MBB alternative for YHWH is 'Yahweh.' Distinct from the generic title 'Lord' (also 'Panginoon') applied to Jesus — set covenant use in small caps to disambiguate. Never a substitute for 'Ama namin.'
The near-universal Filipino term across Catholic and Protestant (MBB) usage. 'Banal na Espiritu' exists but 'Espiritu Santo' is the settled, beloved form.

Anchored to Ang Biblia / MBB (Mt 10:20) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "ang Espiritu ng aming Ama". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)
Standard Filipino Bible title (MBB / Ang Biblia); Jesus' self-designation.
Jesus as the Son; 'Anak' with the article. Correlates naturally with the filial 'Ama' so the Father–Son relation reads as one family.
Christological title; standard Filipino form. Reviewer to confirm whether FHB prefers 'Anak ng Ama' (Son of the Father) in filial contexts vs. the traditional 'ng Diyos.'
MBB's John 3:16 wording. The classic Ang Biblia form 'bugtong na Anak' (only-begotten Son) is also cherished; 'kaisa-isang' fits the contemporary register and the literal 'one and only.'

Anchored to Ang Biblia / MBB (Mt 26:29) — the established rendering this language's church already prays; FHB shifts only the person (our), never the construction. Was: "ang kaharian ng aming Ama". Sweep 2026-07-22 (Kevin: mine the existing translations, don't invent)
Standard Bible spelling in both MBB and Ang Biblia (spoken 'Hesus'; written 'Jesus').
Traditional Filipino form (MBB / Catholic usage). Protestant modern spelling 'Kristo' is an accepted variant; reviewer to set house style.
Standard Filipino transliteration (e.g. Jn 1:41, 4:25 MBB).
Standard Filipino Bible term; 'Bagong Tipan' = New Covenant/Testament. Settled and universally recognized.
Renders Hebrew hesed. 'Wagas' (pure, unwavering) captures covenant constancy; reviewer to weigh vs. 'tapat na pag-ibig' (faithful love), which foregrounds loyalty.
Renders racham / splanchnizomai. 'Habag' is the deep, visceral tenderness (a parent moved for a child), warmer than 'awa' (mercy) — fitting the Father's heart.
Standard Filipino Bible term for both God's righteousness and right standing.
The beloved John 3:16 wording in Filipino; universally recognized.
MBB / Ang Biblia rendering of Jn 3:3,7. 'Isilang na muli' is an accepted synonym; 'ipanganak na muli' is the more common spoken and printed form.
